title: Fix camera permission popup not triggering in CameraPreview

requirements.summary: Replace the camera toggle handler in CameraPreview.tsx to trigger the browser's native permission popup immediately on button click, without any pre-checks or device enumeration, and apply a permanent CSS blur filter to the video element.
acceptanceCriteria: Clicking the camera toggle button immediately triggers the browser's native permission popup without any delay or silent failure.
No `enumerateDevices()` call or any other async operation precedes the `getUserMedia` call in the handler.
On permission grant, the live video feed is displayed inside the component with a permanent CSS blur applied.
The blur filter on the video element is never removed or toggled off under any condition.
On permission denial or any other error, a human-readable error message is shown to the user in the UI.
On component unmount, all media stream tracks are stopped and the stream reference is cleared.
No legacy code paths (enumerateDevices, deviceId constraints, overconstrained retries) remain in the file.

file_operations.description: Completely replace the camera toggle handler implementation: (1) Remove all existing device enumeration, feature detection, and constraint logic; (2) Attach an onClick handler directly on the toggle button that synchronously calls `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({ video: true, audio: false })` as the very first asynchronous operation to ensure the browser permission popup is triggered immediately; (3) Stop all tracks on any existing stream before requesting a new one; (4) On success, assign the stream to `videoRef.current.srcObject` and call `videoRef.current.play()`; (5) On failure, set an error state and display the message 'Camera access was denied. Please allow camera access when the browser prompt appears and try again.' in the UI; (6) Apply a permanent inline CSS blur filter (`filter: 'blur(12px) brightness(0.8) contrast(1.2)'`) to the `<video>` element at all times—never remove or toggle it; (7) Stop all tracks and nullify the stream on component unmount. Strip out all prior code including enumerateDevices calls, deviceId constraints, resolution constraints, feature-detection guards, and any try/catch blocks wrapping getUserMedia before the call is made.
